title: EA Processes for the Agentic Organisation
subtitle: From phased architecture cycles to continuous, event-driven architecture development. How EA processes must evolve when AI deployment velocity outpaces traditional governance cadences.
description: This page covers the Architecture Development Method (ADM) and its evolution for agentic organisations — from phased cycles to continuous architecture, dual-speed development, agent-native process design, and automated architecture discovery.
sources: TOGAF ADM Techniques — FC Part 2, Chapters 2-10
TOGAF Applying the ADM — FC Part 3, Chapters 1-4
TSG Agile Sprints — Applying ADM using Agile
TSG Practitioners' Approach — Practical ADM Application
TSG Architecture Project Management — Integration with PM

sections.label: Process Fundamentals
sections.summary: The Architecture Development Method (ADM) is TOGAF's core process engine — a generic method for developing and managing the lifecycle of an Enterprise Architecture. Critically, it is a reference model of architecture activities, not a mandated waterfall sequence.

subsections.title: The ADM Cycle
subsections.content:
The ADM defines a phased cycle comprising a Preliminary Phase, eight named phases (A through H), and a continuous Requirements Management hub:

PhasePurposeKey Outputs
PreliminaryEstablish architecture capability, define principles, tailor the ADMArchitecture Principles, Tailored Framework
A — VisionHigh-level aspirational vision of capabilities and business valueArchitecture Vision, Statement of Architecture Work
B — BusinessDescribe how the enterprise needs to operate to achieve goalsBusiness Architecture Definition
C — Information SystemsDevelop Data and Application ArchitecturesData & Application Architecture Definitions
D — TechnologyMap logical components onto technology componentsTechnology Architecture Definition
E — OpportunitiesIdentify delivery vehicles and implementation approachImplementation & Migration Strategy
F — Migration PlanningDetailed migration plan with cost/benefit/risk analysisImplementation & Migration Plan
G — Implementation GovernanceArchitectural oversight during implementationArchitecture Compliance Assessments
H — Change ManagementManage change to deployed architectureChange Requests, Updated Architecture
Requirements ManagementContinuous management of requirements across all phasesRequirements Repository

subsections.title: Key Design Characteristics
subsections.content:
TOGAF explicitly states the ADM is "a generic method for architecture development" intended to be "used as a guide and does not mandate a waterfall approach." This is critical but frequently misunderstood.

Reference Model

The ADM is a taxonomy of architecture activities, not a mandated sequence. Phases can be applied selectively based on context.

Iteration Support

Part 3 explicitly supports cycles within phases, across phases, and between phases. Architecture Development and Definition Iterations allow revisiting earlier work.

Landscape Application

The ADM operates at three levels — strategic, segment, and capability — allowing different parts of the enterprise to run at different cadences.

Partitioning

Federating architecture work across organisational boundaries, with different partitions at different phases simultaneously.

Agile Delivery

The TSG on Agile Sprints provides guidance for running ADM phases within sprint cycles, producing usable architecture output incrementally.

Practitioner Flexibility

Four practical scenarios — strategy, portfolio, project, solution delivery — demonstrate selective phase application based on context.

subsections.title: ADM Techniques
subsections.content:
Part 2 provides supporting techniques used across the ADM cycle. These are phase-independent tools drawn upon as needed:

TechniquePurposeAI-Era Extension Needed
Architecture PrinciplesGeneral rules and guidelines for architectureAdd AI-specific principles (agent accountability, model portability)
Stakeholder ManagementIdentify and manage stakeholder concernsAdd AI agents as stakeholders with machine-readable concerns
Architecture PatternsReusable solution templatesAdd multi-agent, RAG, model abstraction, human-in-the-loop patterns
Gap AnalysisIdentify gaps between baseline and targetAdd AI infrastructure, model capability, sovereignty gaps
Migration PlanningTechniques for transition planningAdd AI value progression and dual-speed transitions
Business Transformation ReadinessAssess readiness for changeAdd AI readiness dimensions (infrastructure, data, skills, process)
Risk ManagementIdentify and mitigate risksAdd agent cascade failures, hallucination, adversarial attacks
InteroperabilityEnsure systems work togetherAdd agent-to-agent protocols (MCP, A2A, ACP)

sections.label: The Speed Mismatch
sections.summary: The dominant signal across the research corpus is that EA's phased deliberative approach cannot keep pace with AI deployment velocity. The ADM cycle assumes a cadence measured in quarters or years — AI model lifecycles are months, agent deployment cycles are weeks.

subsections.title: Evidence for the Mismatch
subsections.content:
Six or more independent sources identify this as the most consistently cited barrier to AI-era EA effectiveness:

  • Three sources directly cite TOGAF's phased model as misaligned with AI deployment velocity, arguing that a sequential process introduces structural latency into architecture decisions
  • Architecture must become continuous and event-driven, not phased — shifting from "architecture as a project activity" to "architecture as an always-on capability"
  • Automated architecture discovery from deployment pipelines, cloud APIs, and CMDBs can replace manual data gathering
  • Real-time conformance monitoring means compliance checking happens at deployment time, not at ADM review gates

The acceleration paradox: Organisations with clean, modular architectures gain 30-50% faster AI adoption, but achieving that modularity requires architecture work that itself takes time. Organisations trapped in legacy complexity cannot architect fast enough to benefit from AI that would help them architect faster.

subsections.title: What Breaks in the ADM
subsections.content:
Assumptions That No Longer Hold
  • Architecture has a natural quarterly/yearly cadence — When model lifecycles are months and agent deployment is weeks, quarterly cycles produce decisions based on outdated assumptions
  • Process design precedes technology selection — The B→C→D→E phase ordering assumes processes are designed first, then technology selected. For agent-native processes, both must be co-designed
  • Architecture content is created by humans — Auto-generation from deployment pipelines, CMDBs, and runtime telemetry replaces manual documentation for large portions of the landscape
  • The cycle has a clear start and end — Continuous architecture development has no beginning or end; different concerns run at different cadences simultaneously
  • Outputs are documents — Enterprise as Code requires machine-readable specifications, not just human-readable deliverables
What Survives and Should Be Protected
  • ADM as a reference model — The vocabulary of phases names the right categories of architecture work
  • Iteration model — Cycles within and across phases provide the foundation for continuous architecture
  • Landscape-level application — Strategic/segment/capability levels support different cadences
  • Architecture partitioning — Federated work across boundaries supports "Freedom within a Frame"
  • The technique library — All ADM techniques remain structurally sound; they need AI extension, not replacement
  • Requirements Management as continuous — Already positioned as a hub; precedent for making other activities continuous
  • Practitioners' approach — Selective phase application based on context is the right foundation

subsections.title: Core Tensions
subsections.content:
The research surfaces two significant tensions that practitioners must navigate:

Speed vs. Thoroughness

Process redesign for agent strengths requires deep analysis that takes time, while governance speed demands faster cycles. Resolution: Distinguish between activities that must be fast (technology decisions, compliance, deployment governance) and those that should be deliberate (process redesign, operating model, strategic architecture).

Aspiration vs. Tooling Maturity

The vision for continuous, automated architecture development exceeds current tooling maturity. Most organisations lack the repository completeness and integration infrastructure. Resolution: The path from phased to continuous ADM is itself a multi-year journey — the ADM must reform itself using its current capabilities.

sections.label: Dual-Speed Architecture
sections.summary: Different architecture concerns require different development cadences. Technology architecture operates at deployment speed (days/weeks), business architecture at strategic speed (quarters), and operating model architecture at organisational change speed (months).

subsections.title: The Dual-Speed Operating Model
subsections.content:
ModeScopeCadenceMethod
ContinuousTechnology architecture, deployment compliance, infrastructure decisionsDays to weeksAuto-discovered content, automated compliance, event-driven decisions with exception-based human review
DeliberativeBusiness architecture, strategic architecture, operating model design, agent-native process redesignWeeks to quartersFull ADM phases with sprint-based delivery, compressed cycle times, stakeholder engagement

TOGAF's landscape-level application (strategic/segment/capability) provides the conceptual foundation for this split. The innovation is making the different cadences explicit and designing automation for the continuous mode.

subsections.title: Continuous Mode
subsections.content:
For technology and deployment architecture, the continuous mode operates as an always-on capability:

  • Architecture content auto-discovered from cloud inventory APIs, CI/CD pipeline metadata, service mesh telemetry, and API gateway configurations
  • Compliance checked at deployment time — integrated into CI/CD pipelines, not waiting for ADM review gates
  • Architecture decisions automated for standard patterns — new deployments that conform to approved patterns proceed without human review
  • Exception-based escalation — only novel patterns, significant deviations, or high-risk changes trigger human architect review
  • Drift detection — continuous monitoring alerts when deployed state diverges from target architecture

subsections.title: Deliberative Mode
subsections.content:
For business architecture and strategic decisions, the deliberative mode preserves rigour with compressed timelines:

  • Sprint-based ADM delivery with two-week cycles producing usable architecture output each sprint
  • Full stakeholder engagement through Architecture Vision (Phase A) and iterative review
  • Agent-native process design integrated into Phase B — explicitly asking "augment or redesign?" for each business capability
  • Model selection framework integrated into Phase E — cost/accuracy/latency trade-offs, build-versus-fine-tune decisions
  • Business Transformation Readiness extended with AI dimensions — infrastructure capacity, data quality, process maturity, workforce AI literacy

sections.label: Agent-Native Process Design
sections.summary: Automating existing human-designed processes with AI agents captures only a fraction of available value. BCG's 10/20/70 rule: 70% of AI success depends on people and processes, not algorithms or technology. Processes must be redesigned from scratch for agent capabilities.

subsections.title: Augment vs. Redesign
subsections.content:
The research distinguishes two fundamentally different approaches to applying AI to business processes:

ApproachDescriptionWhen to UseExpected Value
Agent-AugmentedAdd AI to existing human-designed processes — chatbots, copilots, automated data entryProcess is fundamentally human-centric; AI assists specific tasks10-30% efficiency gain
Agent-NativeRedesign the process from scratch for agent capabilities — parallel execution, 24/7 operation, perfect memory, zero-cost replicationProcess can be reimagined without human constraints as the design baseline3-10x throughput, new capabilities

POC fatigue: Organisations running many disconnected AI proofs-of-concept without redesigning underlying processes produce pilot successes that cannot scale. The ADM's Phase E emphasis on identifying individual AI use cases encourages this pattern.

subsections.title: Assessment Framework
subsections.content:
Add to Phase B as a standard technique — for each business capability where agent deployment is considered, assess:

Process Complexity

How complex is the current process? High complexity with many handoffs and exceptions favours redesign.

Agent Capability Fit

How well do agent strengths (parallel, 24/7, perfect memory) map to process requirements?

Change Management Effort

What is the organisational change cost? Augmentation has lower change cost but lower value.

Value Uplift

What is the expected value difference between augmentation and redesign? Justify the higher cost of redesign.

subsections.title: Agent-Native Design Principles
subsections.content:
• Design for parallelism — Agents can execute multiple paths simultaneously; don't force sequential workflows when parallel execution is possible
  • Design for continuous operation — Agents don't have shifts, breaks, or working hours; processes can run 24/7 with human oversight at defined checkpoints
  • Design for perfect memory — Agents can recall every prior interaction and decision; don't rebuild context at each step
  • Design for zero-cost replication — Spinning up additional agent instances costs virtually nothing; design processes that can scale horizontally
  • Design for human escalation — Define clear escalation boundaries where human judgement, empathy, or physical presence is essential
  • Design for observability — Every agent decision must be traceable; design processes with logging, audit trails, and explainability built in

sections.label: New Process Capabilities
sections.summary: Three major new process capabilities are required that have no TOGAF precedent: Continuous Architecture Development, AI-Augmented Architecture Discovery, and Agent-Native Process Design methodology.

subsections.title: Continuous Architecture Development
subsections.content:
An always-on architecture capability that continuously discovers, validates, and updates architecture content, replacing discrete phased cycles with a streaming operating model.

LevelDescription
Level 1ADM applied as phased cycle with quarterly or longer cadence. All content manually created. Compliance checked at milestone reviews.
Level 2Sprint-based ADM delivery with two-week cycles. Some auto-discovered content (infrastructure topology). Compliance partially automated.
Level 3Dual-speed operation established. Technology architecture on continuous cadence. Business and strategic architecture on deliberative cadence with sprint delivery. Most routine decisions automated.
Level 4Full continuous architecture for technology and information systems. Business architecture on fast-cycle sprints. Content 80%+ auto-discovered. Compliance fully automated with exception-based review.
Level 5Architecture as a continuously operating capability with no distinct cycles. All content maintained in real-time. Decisions automated for all standard patterns. Architects focus on novel situations, strategy, and agent-native process design.

subsections.title: AI-Augmented Architecture Discovery
subsections.content:
Automated discovery of architecture content from deployment pipelines, cloud inventory APIs, network topography, runtime telemetry, and service meshes. Replaces manual data gathering for the as-is landscape.

LevelDescription
Level 1All architecture content manually gathered and documented. Repository updated during architecture reviews.
Level 2Some automated discovery for cloud infrastructure (IaC state files, cloud inventory APIs). Other domains still manual.
Level 3Automated discovery for infrastructure, application deployment, and integration topology. Service mesh and API gateway data feeds into repository. Manual effort focused on business architecture.
Level 4Comprehensive discovery across infrastructure, deployment, integration, data flows, and compliance. Near-real-time repository updates. Drift detection alerts on divergence.
Level 5Full digital twin of the enterprise technology landscape maintained automatically. Discovery feeds drive automated compliance, gap analysis, and change impact assessment. Human architects work exclusively at design and strategy level.

subsections.title: Agent-Native Process Design Methodology
subsections.content:
A structured methodology for designing business processes natively for AI agent capabilities rather than automating existing human-designed processes. Includes assessment criteria for augment-versus-redesign decisions.

LevelDescription
Level 1AI applied to existing processes without redesign. "Bolt-on" automation of individual tasks within human-designed workflows.
Level 2Process modifications to accommodate AI (e.g., removing manual data entry). Still fundamentally human-designed processes with AI enhancements.
Level 3Systematic augment-versus-redesign assessment for each business capability. Some processes redesigned from scratch. Phase B explicitly addresses the distinction.
Level 4Agent-native process design is the default starting point where agent execution is feasible. Human-centric design applied only where human judgement, empathy, or physical presence is essential.
Level 5Dynamic process design — processes adapt in real-time based on agent performance and context. Human architects define objectives, constraints, and guardrails; agents optimise execution within those boundaries.

sections.label: Practitioner Guidance
sections.summary: Concrete actions for EA practitioners, structured by time horizon. These recommendations bridge the gap between TOGAF's phased ADM and the continuous, agent-native process development that agentic organisations require.

phases.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Measure your architecture cycle time — calculate elapsed time from request to approved decision for your last five engagements. Compare against AI deployment cadence. If architecture takes longer than deployment, you are performing architecture review theatre.

Classify your architecture backlog by required cadence — deployment-speed (days/weeks), organisational-change-speed (months), or strategic-speed (quarters/years). This reveals where continuous architecture is most urgent.
Identify one architecture content area for automated discovery — select the area that stales fastest (typically infrastructure topology) and connect it to an automated data source.
Add agent-native process design as a Phase B technique — when developing business architecture for AI initiatives, explicitly ask: 'Should we augment the existing process or redesign from scratch?'
Coordinate with governance changes — faster architecture development without faster governance approval produces bottlenecks.

phases.title: Near-Term Actions
actions: Implement dual-speed architecture development — continuous mode for technology architecture with automated discovery and compliance; deliberative mode for business and strategic architecture with sprint-based delivery.
Extend Phase E with AI-specific solution selection criteria — model selection guidance covering cost/accuracy/latency trade-offs, build-versus-fine-tune decisions, vendor lock-in assessment.
Add AI readiness dimensions to Business Transformation Readiness Assessment — infrastructure capacity, data quality, process maturity, workforce AI literacy, weighted by the 10/20/70 rule.
Develop AI-specific architecture patterns — multi-agent orchestration, model abstraction layer, protocol abstraction (MCP/A2A/ACP), human-in-the-loop, RAG, model routing and gateway patterns.
Create agent deployment sequencing guidance — define how to sequence agent rollout based on process readiness, data availability, and organisational change capacity.

phases.title: Medium-Term Actions
actions: Transition to continuous architecture for technology domains — automated discovery, CI/CD-integrated compliance, automated standard pattern decisions, exception-based human review.
Produce machine-readable architecture deliverables — architecture principles, standards, policies in YAML/JSON/policy-as-code formats, serving both human architects and automated governance tools.
Pilot agent-native process redesign on one value stream — apply zero-based redesign, compare against traditional Phase B → Phase E output, refine the methodology.
Integrate the ADM with AI value progression planning — Phase One (productivity/cost savings) requires different architecture emphasis than Phase Two (revenue/growth).
Develop an architecture development playbook for multi-agent systems — agent decomposition, interaction design, failure handling, testing, deployment, and observability.

sections.label: Phase-by-Phase Evolution
sections.summary: Each ADM phase requires specific adaptations for agentic organisations. This section maps the key changes per phase — what stays, what changes, and what's new.

subsections.title: Phase A — Architecture Vision
subsections.content:
What Changes
  • Architecture Vision must include an AI strategy assessment — where is the organisation on the AI value progression (productivity → revenue)?
  • Stakeholder analysis must include AI/ML teams, data science, and AI ethics/governance roles
  • Scope definition must address agent boundaries — which processes are candidates for agent-native redesign?
  • The Vision document must be partially machine-readable to feed automated governance tools
What's New
  • Agent opportunity mapping — identify business capabilities where agent deployment could create step-change value
  • AI readiness pre-assessment — quick assessment of infrastructure, data, skills, and process readiness before committing to full architecture development
  • Model landscape scan — survey available AI models and their applicability to identified opportunities

subsections.title: Phase B — Business Architecture
subsections.content:
What Changes
  • Process modelling must include the augment-versus-redesign assessment for each capability
  • Business scenarios must include agent scenarios — how agents participate, what happens when they fail, human-agent handoffs
  • Operating model design must produce executable specifications (Enterprise as Code), not just documentation
  • Organisation design must account for human-agent teams and new roles (Agent Owner, Prompt Engineer, AI Ethics Officer)
What's New
  • Zero-based process design — redesign selected processes from scratch for agent capabilities
  • Agent capability mapping — map agent strengths to business capability requirements
  • 10/20/70 assessment — weight initiative planning: 10% algorithms, 20% technology, 70% people and processes

subsections.title: Phases C & D — Information Systems & Technology
subsections.content:
What Changes
  • Data Architecture must address AI training data, vector databases, knowledge graphs, and data sovereignty constraints
  • Application Architecture must treat AI agents as first-class application components with defined interfaces, SLAs, and lifecycle management
  • Technology Architecture must include AI infrastructure — GPU compute, model serving platforms, agent runtime environments
  • Technology standards must evolve at AI speed — model recommendations may have shelf lives of months, not years
What's New
  • AI infrastructure readiness assessment — only 22% of organisations have infrastructure ready for AI at scale
  • Model abstraction layer design — architectural pattern to avoid model lock-in and enable portability
  • Agent communication architecture — MCP, A2A, ACP protocol selection and integration patterns

subsections.title: Phases E & F — Opportunities, Solutions & Migration
subsections.content:
What Changes
  • Solution selection must include model selection framework — cost/accuracy/latency trade-offs, build vs. fine-tune vs. prompt decisions
  • Migration planning must account for AI value progression — different architecture emphasis for Phase 1 (productivity) vs. Phase 2 (revenue)
  • Build-versus-buy now includes build vs. fine-tune vs. prompt vs. buy for AI components
  • Cost-benefit analysis must include AI-specific costs — inference costs, model retraining, data preparation, sovereignty compliance
What's New
  • Agent deployment sequencing — prioritise by process readiness, data availability, and change capacity
  • Model portfolio strategy — right-size models to tasks, design for interchange as the market evolves
  • Sovereignty-aware deployment planning — map AI workloads to deployment constraints based on data residency and jurisdictional rules

subsections.title: Phases G & H — Implementation Governance & Change
subsections.content:
What Changes
  • Implementation Governance becomes continuous automated compliance — integrated into CI/CD pipelines, checking at deployment time
  • Architecture Compliance Assessments become automated for standard patterns with exception-based human review
  • Change Management must handle AI-speed change — model updates, agent reconfigurations, and capability improvements that occur weekly, not annually
  • Architecture contracts must be machine-readable and automatically enforced
What's New
  • Automated drift detection — continuous monitoring of deployed state vs. target architecture with automated alerting
  • Agent performance governance — monitoring agent accuracy, cost, latency, and escalation rates against defined thresholds
  • Model lifecycle governance — tracking model versions, deprecation timelines, and replacement planning
